title: Power
whatItIs:
Power is the ability to shape outcomes, allocate resources, set rules, and influence the behaviour of others. It shows up in governments, companies, families, networks, and culture.

The topic is not only about coercion. It is also about legitimacy, persuasion, agenda-setting, and the ability to coordinate people toward a goal.
whyItMatters:
Power matters because it quietly shapes nearly every outcome people care about: safety, pay, access, status, and freedom. If you do not see power, you will misunderstand why decisions are made.

It also matters because people often confuse formal authority with actual influence. The two are related, but they are not the same thing.
keyIdeas: Power can be formal or informal
Influence is often hidden in process
Legitimacy matters as much as force
frontier: The frontier is how power works in networked systems: platforms, AI tools, media ecosystems, and transnational institutions. That makes power harder to see and easier to misread.
openQuestions: Where does real power sit in a given system?
How do formal roles differ from actual influence?
Can power be made more accountable without being paralysed?
